subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can plan and do experiments to show that vibrating things make sound and sound can make things vibrate.
Wave Properties
Your child plans and does tests to show that vibrating things make sound and sound can make things vibrate.
Your child explains with evidence how vibrating things can make sound and how sound can make things vibrate.
Your child can gather and share information to compare mechanical waves and electromagnetic waves and how they are used in real life.
Your child can use models to show that vibrating things make sound and sound can make things vibrate.
Your child can investigate how vibrating things make sound and how sound can make things vibrate.
Your child understands that sound comes from vibrating things and can also make things vibrate.
canonical_name: Grade 1 Science: Waves — Sound and Vibrations
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on the relationship between vibration and sound, but one standard introduces electromagnetic waves, which is a minor extension beyond the primary focus.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can give examples of rules and laws in Louisiana.
Your child can explain why water is important and how to stay safe near it.
Your child can explain how citizens help their community and state by following rules and paying taxes.
Your child can describe when there is an emergency and who to ask for help.
Your child can identify healthy foods and activities that help keep the body strong.
Your child can look at information from different websites to find medical facts that might not be true.
Your child can find health information from home, school, and community sources.
Your child can write medical stories using correct spelling, grammar, and punctuation.
canonical_name: Grade 1 Science: Health and Safety — Community Rules and Personal Well-being
coherence_reason: The standards cover multiple distinct topics: rules/laws, water safety, citizenship, emergency response, nutrition, and health information literacy.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can observe and describe shapes by their important features, sort and draw them, and begin learning about fractions by dividing shapes.
Your child can compare groups of people by age, religion, ethnicity, disabilities, and gender equality.
Your child knows that metals share traits like conducting electricity and heat, and that some metals are pure while others are mixtures.
Your child can identify and draw shapes by their features, like how many angles or equal sides they have, including triangles, quadrilaterals, pentagons, hexagons, and cubes.
Your child notices and reacts to different things they see, hear, or feel.
Your child notices differences in objects by looking at their size, shape, or color.
Your child can recognize a familiar object when it looks bigger through a magnifying glass.
Your child looks carefully to tell objects apart by size, shape, color, or feel.
canonical_name: Grade 1 Science: Properties of Objects — Observing and Describing Attributes
coherence_reason: Most standards are about observing and describing physical properties of objects, but some are about geometry (shapes and fractions) and one about social groups, which are distinct topics.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child knows that a planet moves around the Sun because of the Sun’s gravity pulling on it.
Your child knows uneven heating of Earth causes air to move in currents.
Your child understands that air pressure from Earth’s atmosphere gets weaker as you go higher and pushes equally in all directions at any spot.
Your child shows that pushing or pulling can change an object's motion.
Your child can show and understand that pushing or pulling makes things move.
Your child shows and understands that pushing or pulling makes objects move.
Your child can push something to make it move.
Your child knows that pushing or pulling changes how an object moves.
canonical_name: Grade 1 Science: Forces and Motion — Pushes and Pulls
coherence_reason: There are two distinct concepts: some standards describe gravity and motion of planets, while others focus on push/pull forces and basic motion.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child will see that objects fall down when dropped.
Your child can tell the difference between fast and slow actions.
Your child shows and talks about different ways objects move, like straight, zigzag, back-and-forth, round, fast, or slow.
Your child shows and knows that objects can move up and down, straight, or back and forth.
Your child can show and name different ways objects move, such as up and down, straight, or back and forth.
Your child learns that Earth's gravity pulls objects down even when nothing is touching them.
Your child can show where an object is before and after it falls.
Your child can tell which events happen fast and which happen slowly.
canonical_name: Grade 1 Science: Motion — Ways Objects Move and Effects of Gravity
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on describing and demonstrating different types of motion, with a minor emphasis on gravity's role in causing downward motion.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can plan and do simple tests to see what happens when different materials block a beam of light.
Your child explores how magnifiers help us see small things by making them look bigger.
Your child plans and does an experiment to see what happens when different materials block a beam of light.
Your child can guess ways to melt an ice cube faster, like putting it in the sun or shining light on it.
Your child plans and does an experiment to see how shadows change when objects move closer or farther from light.
Your child asks questions to find and compare different sources of light.
Your child will plan and do tests to see how objects made of different materials affect a beam of light and guess how similar objects will change the light.
Your child can test how different materials affect a beam of light when placed in its path.
canonical_name: Grade 1 Science: Light — Properties and Interactions with Materials
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on light's interaction with materials, shadows, and sources, but magnifiers and melting ice introduce minor variations.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can observe and explain that things in the dark can only be seen when light shines on them.
Your child can name one good and one bad effect of sunlight.
Your child understands that sunlight can be good for us but can also cause harm.
Your child understands that sunlight can be helpful and sometimes harmful.
Your child knows that sunlight warms things and gives light.
Your child can recognize one good and one bad effect of sunlight.
Your child knows that magnifiers make things look bigger.
Your child knows that sunlight can warm things and give light.
canonical_name: Grade 1 Science: Light — Sunlight and Its Effects
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on sunlight's positive and negative effects, but one addresses magnifiers, which is only loosely related to the main sunlight concept.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child understands what passwords are, why we use them, and can make strong passwords to keep devices and information safe.
Your child will act kindly and responsibly when working with others online.
Your child will keep their password secret and log off devices when finished.
Your child works nicely and responsibly with others when using electronic communication.
Your child knows to give credit when using other people’s ideas or work in their programs.
Your child understands why it’s important to keep login info private and how to log off devices properly.
Your child can explain why people use passwords.
Your child uses correct capitalization, punctuation, and spelling when writing.
canonical_name: Grade 1 Science: Digital Citizenship — Passwords, Privacy, and Online Responsibility
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on online safety, password security, and responsible digital behavior, but one standard about writing mechanics is unrelated.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can save, find, change, and delete information on a computer and knows this information is called data.
Your child can name and describe what common computer parts do, like the mouse, keyboard, or screen.
Your child can break a problem into smaller steps and write out clear instructions to solve it.
Your child can talk about simple problems with computer parts or programs using the right words.
Your child understands that computers use numbers or symbols to store and change information.
Your child can create simple computer programs that follow steps in order and repeat actions to share ideas or solve problems.
Your child can explain how industrial growth, city life, new communication, and cultural changes affected life in the U.S. from the late 1800s to World War I.
Your child can design and build a tool that uses light or sound to help people communicate from far away.
canonical_name: Grade 1 Science: Computers and Communication — Data, Devices, and Problem Solving
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on computers, data, and basic programming, but one standard is about historical changes in the U.S. and another about engineering a communication tool, which are distinct topics.
split_topics: Basic Computer Literacy and Programming Concepts
Historical Effects of Industrial Growth and Communication Advances

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can use materials to create a solution to a problem by copying how plants or animals use their body parts to survive and grow.
Your child knows that plants have leaves.
Your child can point out the main parts of a plant, like the stem, roots, leaves, and flowers.
Your child knows what a plant’s leaf and flower look like.
Your child can recognize the leaf and flower parts of a plant.
Your child can name the leaf, flower, and stem parts of a plant.
Your child can point out the leaf, flower, and stem parts of a plant.
Your child uses materials to design a way to solve a problem by copying how plants or animals use their body parts to survive and grow.
canonical_name: Grade 1 Science: Plants — Identifying Plant Parts
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on identifying plant parts, but two standards are about biomimicry and problem-solving inspired by plants or animals, which is a distinct concept.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can observe young plants and animals and explain how they are similar but not exactly like their parents.
Your child can read and watch videos to learn how parents and their babies behave in ways that help the babies survive.
Your child learns how traits are passed from parents to offspring and how they can be different.
Your child understands that chromosomes carry DNA with instructions for traits, and cells use different genes in different ways.
Your child can observe that plants and animals look like their parents but also have differences.
Recognize one's own parents.
Your child can match baby animals to their parents.
Your child understands that baby plants and animals have parents.
canonical_name: Grade 1 Science: Heredity — Parents and Offspring
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on similarities and differences between parents and offspring, but one standard introduces chromosomes and DNA, which is more advanced than the others.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can locate all the continents on a map or globe.
Your child can tell the difference between natural resources and things made by people.
Your child can tell if the outside temperature feels warm or cold.
Your child can tell which things are alive and which are not.
Your child can identify rocks, water, and living things outside.
Your child can identify rocks, water, and living things around them.
Your child can tell if something is living or not by checking if it needs food or water.
Your child can recognize rocks and living things in their surroundings.
canonical_name: Grade 1 Science: Earth and Environment — Living, Nonliving, and Natural Resources
coherence_reason: The standards include identification of living vs. nonliving things, natural resources, and a separate standard about locating continents and weather, indicating related but distinct subtopics.
split_topics: Living vs. Nonliving Things and Natural Resources

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can look at test results from two things made to solve the same problem and talk about what each does well and not so well.
Your child can ask questions and gather information to understand a problem that people want to fix by making a new or better tool.
Your child can suggest a way to solve a problem by looking at information and thinking carefully, both alone and with others.
Developing Possible Solutions
Your child can work on improving a solution by testing and making it better.
Your child can clearly define an engineering problem and set limits to focus on solving it.
Your child shows that there are many ways to solve a design problem and none are perfect.
Your child learns that anyone can create and make new things.
canonical_name: Grade 1 Science: Engineering Design — Defining and Solving Problems
coherence_reason: All standards focus on the process of defining, developing, evaluating, and iterating solutions to engineering problems, appropriate for early elementary engineering design.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can draw or build a simple model to show how the shape of something helps it do its job.
Your child can make simple models or designs to show how things work or to solve a problem.
Your child can create simple charts or pictures to show information, alone or with others.
Your child can solve questions alone and with others using models.
Your child draws pictures to show what they learned from an experiment.
Modeling and Simulations
Your child can show their thoughts and stories step-by-step using writing, photos, or drawings.
Your child can explain how models like globes or maps show real things.
canonical_name: Grade 1 Science: Modeling — Using and Creating Models to Represent Information
coherence_reason: Most standards are about using models to represent scientific ideas, processes, or information, but a few touch more generally on using models for communication or storytelling beyond science.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can collect information and show it in different ways, like pictures, charts, or graphs.
Your child can look at charts or graphs, spot patterns, and use them to make guesses about what might happen next.
Your child can use calendars and timelines to learn about events from the past.
Your child can compare old and current maps of Alabama to learn about its weather, land, waterways, transportation, politics, economy, land use, and population.
Your child can find important physical features like ocean currents, winds, forests, rivers, and mountains that affected exploration and settlement in the Americas.
Your child will collect and organize information using drawings, numbers, words, symbols, or simple charts.
Your child can compare how different countries and regions trade with each other.
Your child can find land, water, and other features on maps and globes.
canonical_name: Grade 1 Science: Data and Maps — Collecting, Organizing, and Interpreting Information
coherence_reason: The standards are related to information gathering and interpretation, but they mix general data collection/representation with map/geography skills and basic social studies concepts, suggesting two distinct sub-concepts.
split_topics: Data Collection and Interpretation (charts, graphs, timelines)
Geography and Map Skills (maps, physical features, trade)

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can watch and record how daylight changes during the year and make a graph to show what they find.
Your child can use what they see about the sun, moon, stars, and tides to describe patterns that happen again and again.
Your child describes how things around us can change or stay the same because of different conditions.
Your child can find important lines on maps and globes like the prime meridian, equator, Tropics of Capricorn and Cancer, International Date Line, and lines of latitude and longitude.
Your child can name important events and celebrations that happen in your town and in Alabama.
Your child knows the Sun is the biggest object in the solar system and is mostly made of hydrogen and helium.
Your child knows the solar system includes Earth, the Moon, the Sun, eight other planets with their moons, and smaller objects like asteroids and comets.
Earth's Place in the Universe
canonical_name: Grade 1 Science: Earth's Place in the Universe — Patterns of Sun, Moon, and Stars
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on astronomical patterns and the solar system, but some reference geography and local events, indicating two distinct subtopics.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Career Research
Your child asks 'how do you know?' when it makes sense to do so.
Your child asks questions about a science experiment.
Your child asks for information about the world around them.
Your child can help add observations to group notes.
Your child keeps simple records like drawings or notes about science activities.
Your child asks questions about nature, explores with others, and explains what they find out.
Your child can ask a question about a science experiment.
canonical_name: Grade 1 Science: Scientific Inquiry — Asking Questions and Recording Observations
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on asking questions and making/recording observations, though 'Career Research' is only loosely related.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child explains how the parts of objects, animals, or systems help them do their jobs.
Your child understands how different parts of the brain have special jobs and what happens if those parts get hurt.
Your child knows that many living things have special parts that help move materials around their bodies.
Your child understands that living things use feedback systems to keep their bodies stable and respond to changes around them.
Your child understands that kidneys remove waste from the blood and turn it into urine, which is stored in the bladder.
Your child knows that cellular respiration breaks down food and oxygen to release energy needed for body functions like keeping warm.
Your child knows the steps of digestion and how the mouth, esophagus, stomach, small intestine, large intestine, and colon each help break down food.
Your child learns about the parts of plants and animals and how they work.
canonical_name: Grade 1 Science: Structures and Functions — Parts and Their Roles in Living and Nonliving Systems
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on the relationship between parts and their functions in living and nonliving systems, but some delve into specific organ systems or feedback mechanisms, creating minor variation.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child knows water vapor moves through the air, forms fog or clouds, and falls as rain, hail, sleet, or snow.
Your child understands that most of Earth's water is salty and found in the oceans, which cover most of the planet's surface.
Your child knows where the water used by their local community comes from.
Your child understands that when water evaporates, it becomes vapor in the air and can turn back into liquid or ice when cooled.
Your child can observe and understand that plants and animals need water.
Your child can observe and understand that plants and animals need both water and food.
Your child can observe and recognize that all plants and animals, including people, need air, water, food, and space to live.
Your child sees and knows that plants and animals need water.
canonical_name: Grade 1 Science: Water — Water Cycle and Living Things' Needs
coherence_reason: The standards mix two sub-concepts: the water cycle/processes and water as a necessity for living things.
split_topics: Water Cycle and States of Water
Water as a Basic Need for Living Things

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can name scientists and engineers like Katherine Johnson and Sally Ride and learn what they do.
Your child earned credit in an elective class that encourages civic participation.
Your child can name different types of energy and describe properties of materials.
Your child understands how science or new inventions can help people.
Engineering Design
Your child knows that during chemical reactions, atoms rearrange to make new substances with different properties.
Your child can use thinking skills to solve real science and engineering problems.
Your child understands that models and simulations can help solve real science and engineering problems.
canonical_name: Grade 1 Science: Scientific Practices — Scientists, Engineers, and Problem Solving
coherence_reason: The standards cluster around understanding what scientists and engineers do and solving problems, but include unrelated concepts like types of energy, material properties, chemical reactions, and civic participation.
split_topics: Famous scientists and engineers and what they do
Problem solving in science and engineering
Types of energy and properties of materials

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can identify reasons why people moved to and settled in North America and what happened because of it.
Your child understands how American Indian cultures in North America were different based on where they lived, their resources, communities, economies, and beliefs.
Your child can identify important national people and celebrations that show values like fairness, justice, and helping others.
Your child understands what rights people have and what they should do as citizens in your community and state.
Your child understands how feelings and habits about money, saving, and work affect spending choices.
Your child learns about different governments from the past and today in Europe and the Americas and understands the rights and duties of people in those systems.
Your child can identify traditions and contributions from different cultures in your community and state.
Your child can describe ways people have changed the environment in Alabama and around the world.
canonical_name: Grade 1 Social Studies: Communities — Culture, Citizenship, and Change
coherence_reason: The standards cover related but distinct sub-concepts: migration/settlement, cultural differences, citizenship/rights, economic choices, government, cultural contributions, and human-environment interaction.
